title: Pre-install and post-install hooks (like helm) support for podman kube play
body:
### Feature request description

Hi, first I would like to thank you for the `podman kube play` feature in podman, will probably write a post on it, how we used it, to run our kubernetes deployments locally on podman, which really increases iteration times and makes deployment more accessible to developers which are not familiar with Kubernetes.

One thing missing is that sometimes you need to start certain things before others, similar to initContainer, just on the whole deploy.yaml scope, not on individual deployments.

For this helm has some labels:
pre-install     Executes after templates are rendered, but before any resources are created in Kubernetes
post-install	Executes after all resources are loaded into Kubernetes
pre-delete	Executes on a deletion request before any resources are deleted from Kubernetes
post-delete	Executes on a deletion request after all of the release's resources have been deleted
pre-upgrade	Executes on an upgrade request after templates are rendered, but before any resources are updated
...

I think that the most important ones would be pre-install and post-install, so if you would define a resources and add:
```yaml
metadata:
   annotations:
      podman.io/hook: pre-install
```

Podman would respect this labels and first create resources with pre-install and last resources with post-install labels.

### Suggest potential solution

As long as podman processes the deploy.yaml file sequentially (which goes against this issue: #24096), implementation of this feature would really be quite trivial I think. All it would need to be done is to process the yaml file, create resources from it and then order them according to the hooks, first all pre-install, then everything non-labeled and at the end post-install resources.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

There are currently two alternatives I know of, for running logic pre-install:
- put the "global init logic" on all deployment/pod resources, so which ever is created first, will run it (and ensure this logic is idempotent) - this is really cumbersome
- if you only have deployment resources and no pod resources, you can create one pod init resource, which will always run before deployment, because helm obeys this [kind resources ordering logic](https://github.com/helm/helm/blob/main/pkg/release/v1/util/kind_sorter.go)

Similarly for running post-install hook, you would need to find a resource which is always rendered last.

For this approaches to work, I assume running the podman kube play command in the following way: `helm template your_chart | podman kube play -`, where helm then applies it's ordering logic.

### Additional context

Podman kube play is an awesome feature making podman unique among other container runtimes as far as I know of. The more it's like kubectl, the easier it will be to writing helm charts, and kubernetes deployments in a way that they can be run locally without kubernetes and with podman alone, which in my eyes is great as:
- we don't always want to deploy our apps in kube environement
- helps immensly when developing/testing deploys locally
- you don't need to know kubernetes to run/create a deployment and makes a flatter learning curve to come from containers to kubernetes

comments.body:
If I understand correctly, the request is about the apply order of the Kubernetes resources in a YAML file. In general, for `kube play`, we tend to be as consistent as possible with kubectl and Kubernetes behavior. And for this use case, kubectl provides a couple of mechanisms to have a predictable order of how the resources are created (apart from the initcontainer that you have mentioned):
- the resource type: some resources, like namespaces, are always created first, and services and pods are created later
- the file name: `kubectl apply -f <dir>` applies the YAMLs in `<dir>` in file names's alphabetical order

Wouldn't those mechanisms work for your `podman kube play` scenarios?

I see, being alined with `kube apply` does make a lot of sense. As we rely on helm, which generates resources in its own order which are then feeded to podman the `podman kube play <dir>` doesnt really work.

> I see, being alined with `kube apply` does make a lot of sense. As we rely on helm, which generates resources in its own order which are then feeded to podman the `podman kube play <dir>` doesnt really work.

Got it. Supporting `podman kube play <dir>` makes a lot of sense. Could you update this issue to reflect that rather than introducing pre-install and post-install hooks?

Sory, maybe I was a little unclear, wanted to say that I understand being is sync with `kubectl apply` make the most sense, but that with helm the `podman kube play <dir>` still would **not** work as  helm outputs a single file (and not a directory).
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
For our needs we had to create a helm charts generator tool and then with this tool we can then arrange resources in the order we need them to be.
